speaker: interviewer
text: Zvi Michaeli. Zvi, will you continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: While we were already on the edge, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he says to me these words-- you will survive and retaliate. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Ah, I have no time to think that whatever it means, it's true. Whatever it says, it's true. But how could it be. I'm in front of the bed. I'm in front of the grave. And the machine guns start to play. And the machine guns start to shoot. When they cut mine-- when he told me the words, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother was so tight up to him. He didn't like to look and to see what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was on his lap, sat. And he put my arm my neck-- his arm on my neck. When the shooting start, the machine guns get off. When I feel, like, he gave me a push in, into the grave. But this bullet-- probably, his arm saved me, came to up here, to my neck. You see the scar? So the bullet just hit my upper skin. But I was starting to bleeding too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], I fall in. He fall in on me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I hear him-- hollowing. His blood was all on me. Probably he got, in chest, shot. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was-- he was laying on me. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I don't know how many times, 5 minutes, 10 minutes. But I'm still conscious. I know what's going on. I feel I am not dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought to myself, if this is death, it's not so bad. I-- [SOBBING] I'm still alive. And-- [CRYING] for a longer time, [SOBBING] I feel this body on me. And [SOBBING] he got heavier and heavier and heavier. [SOBBING] I feel I'm choking. I can't take him anymore. [CRYING] And I feel his blood all over me. I feel like I'm warm, but heavy. [SOBBING] Then I start to figure, no, I'm not dead. And I'm not alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] Oy. [CRYING] I'm sorry. I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: Take a tissue. Have a drink of water.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- when my feelings-- I feel good with him. But I feel good. Even, he is heavy, I feel his closeness to me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I couldn't take it anymore, his weight. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tried to slide out. When I slide out, in way, other corpse, other dead people. They wouldn't let me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was hard to slide out from him. But I managed somehow. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING] [? Heaven. ?] [CRYING] Oy. [CRYING] Oy, my God, my [INAUDIBLE]. [CRYING] I-- I slide out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I managed somehow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I feel mine whole body's full with blood, and it's drying. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I feel I'm not dead. I am not alive. But I came-- I got more strength every minute. Conscious thought come back to me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So let's be quiet and in hiding, and not to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not to give anybody a sign that somebody's alive here. Because I hear every time screaming or yelling or crying, they used to come. When we hear single shots, they used to finish them. Conscious came back to me, fully conscious. But I feel blood, a little. I touch, I feel, it's not my father's blood. Because my father's blood was already dry. Fresh.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I feel. And then I put my finger in. And I saw it went over that. I'm not dying. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already a little before dark, before we were the last group to [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. I heard a lot comments around me. I think it was talking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they all went away, probably. I didn't look around myself. I couldn't look from the grave up. I could see only the skies. I couldn't see what surround me. Little by little, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I pull over to the edge, lifted up mine head. When I saw-- except on the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on the clothes, on pile of clothes-- it's no action anymore. They were busy with selecting the clothes, of schlepping the clothes, stealing the clothes, whatever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I lift up myself. And then I start to run. Run, not looking back, what happened, [INAUDIBLE] [? let ?] [? them ?] shoot me while I'm running. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a little downhill. It was easy to run for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't feel that time to run away, to escape, to hide from somebody there, not to shoot me. I don't care. I just run. I just run where nobody's shooting after me. Nobody's running after me. Nobody's looking after me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] When I run away. It was starting a little forest. Not a too big forest. No, a small-- no, woods or bushes that is a little-- gives you a little shelter, little hiding place. And then I stop. Not because I was tired. But start looking around, where I am and what to do. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Took a rest, I breathe for a couple minutes. And I figure, I'll go back to [INAUDIBLE], to the same farmers when I run away, when I put the German in between the buildings, the way I know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I start to go, naked. Naked except the cover-- the only cover I got is the blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is the-- is the blood?

speaker: interviewee
text: The blood of my father, of my brother, of [INAUDIBLE]. They were surrounding me all. I remember they were. It was so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, flooding the blood like water on me. And I was  covered up over with blood. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got the diarrhea right away. When I tried to sit down to make, I couldn't bend my knees. Because the knees, from here up here, was full with blood. And that became like a skin on the top. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing it the way I could-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I couldn't stop. My stomach was running and running. I couldn't sit down. But who cares? I am free. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Going [INAUDIBLE] [? half ?] a kilometer into the forest, thinking that I'm going to [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Three Poles coming against me, in the direction of Ejszyszki, and I am on the other direction. And they saw me naked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, [NON-ENGLISH], a Jew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Where are you going? I'm running away. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One says, you go back where you belonged. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I say-- the other two were much a little softer. So they kept him. They grabbed him by the arm. Because he grabbed me by the other arm. He said, you go back to belong.

speaker: interviewee
text: They grabbed him. And they tell him in Polish, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. Leave him. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And he-- this guy was the older one, the older one-- the oldest one from these two. He said, no. And they tell him in Polish, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] if they'll bring the Jew back, they'll get a lot-- a lot of belongings from them, a lot of things from them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I understand what he is telling him. So I say, but you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you-- look at the way I look, full with blood. Doesn't it mind you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jesus Christ, he came off the cross full with blood. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am like him. They kill me. They was going to kill me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But leave me alone. Don't I look with him, like him, he went off the cross? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? From where? ?] I don't from where it came to me, this idea to tell him. Because I saw on his neck, a cross, a big cross for-- of this guy. So I said, look. When Jesus also on this cross here, I say, doesn't it look like how Jesus came off the cross? You want me to get-- take me back to the-- to the killers, to the murderers?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't say a word. He  lets me go. He said, go. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I went to the [INAUDIBLE]. When she saw me, she faint, [INAUDIBLE]. She was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a girl, not married-- not married, but older, maybe 40, 45 years old. She was in good relationship with my mother. She used to consult and talk with my mother about everyday life. She was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she was without a mother. Her mother died in our house when she was a girl, from [INAUDIBLE] 10 years before. And my mother consoled her. Another brother, she has, a-- a younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Polish. Polish girl. She lived on the-- on the farm, 5 kilometer away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She took me in. She washed off my wounds. She washed me off, my blood. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She took care of me. I was there in the barn. She kept me in the barn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I ask her go to Radun, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] look for Judith. And I gave her the address where I left Judith a week ago, a couple days ago, four days ago, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with a letter in Yiddish writing, to Judy. [? I'm waiting, ?] thinking that Judy is with [INAUDIBLE]. And I left her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She went. She came to [INAUDIBLE]. Judith wasn't there. And [INAUDIBLE] answered me in Yiddish writing that Judith went to Lida-- the county's city from Radun-- to organize some papers in order to be able to register in Radun. They didn't want to register in Radun. Because the reason, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I start to figure what I know, that they were looking for her in Ejszyszki.

speaker: interviewer
text: So maybe the reputation there, they don't want-- the Judenrat don't want to be any trouble with the German authority. So-- even the German doesn't know that she is [? Judeo ?] and she is here. But they don't want to take a chance, and they didn't register. So she went to Lida. She went with the Russian-- when they occupied Lida-- in Lida, to work for the Russians there in an office. So she has there, some connections. So she figures she'll came there. She'll go there. And she'll find some connections [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and register in Radun [? return ?] later. Because at that time, the German bombed Lida while they were starting the war. All the refugees from Lida, their houses was bombed.

speaker: interviewee
text: They got from the [NON-ENGLISH] commissar, a permission to settle in neighboring [NON-ENGLISH] in Radun. So was there about 40 families from Lida, official to be registered. So she went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she brought a paper that she's a Lida citizen, residence from Lida, with a false name, which she brought to the Judenrat in Radun. Oh, no, let me restart. When that had happened, when she went to Lida to get the documents, [INAUDIBLE] came with [INAUDIBLE], led her to [INAUDIBLE], where I left her. And [INAUDIBLE] had in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? She is ?] [INAUDIBLE] survive, unfortunately for myself. But Judith went to Lida to arrange some papers to be registered in Lida. So that mean in this present time, when I came to [INAUDIBLE], she went to Lida, to arrange the documents. When I'm waiting, that was Thursday. That was Wednesday night. And Thursday, I send her to Radun. I wait, Friday. Because I figure, it will take her another day or two. She take care of my wound. It wasn't too bad, not too deep shot. Just luckily, it was just in the skin. And my father, saved me. He put his arm my-- on my neck. So the bullet went into his arm. [SIGHS] Saturday, I go to Radun. I dressed up like a peasant. She took me 1 kilometer, [INAUDIBLE], on-- on-- on horse and wagon with hay. Under the hay, she covered me. 1 kilometer, I'm coming into Radun [INAUDIBLE]. I'm going in, into the Judenrat, to tell them what happened with the Ejszyszki Jews. I'm going to tell them what happened when I was-- Wednesday-- in the group, in the last group. I took off the bandage. I show them what's happened. I got shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's happened to the rest of them? It's only the women [INAUDIBLE], probably they're going to kill them. They killed them, too. And I knew already.

speaker: interviewee
text: But [INAUDIBLE] went to Ejszyszki, went to [INAUDIBLE] for Radun, when she went back-- through Ejszyszki back to here-- to see what's happened with the women. The women was already, all day Thursday, in execution. And a couple-- a couple groups, remain on Friday. And that was the end of the all women, Thursday and Friday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was with the last group on Wednesday night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came to the Judenrat. First, to the Judenrat, I got in. I say what's happened. And I tell them the story, what happened. I ran away from the graves. [SIGHS] It's very sad to tell. But I got to tell the truth. The head of the Judenrat from Radun, Noach Delinksy-- his name was Noach Delinksy-- stood up [? and ?] [? he ?] [? says, ?] And he give me a [INAUDIBLE] in my face. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he says, we don't like to see you here and to panic all the community. I don't want you, to see you anymore here. Get lost. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I got lost. I have-- I have no choice. If they wouldn't register me, I have no place. So nobody would let me in, even to only sleep. But anyway, I got to [INAUDIBLE], to look for Judith. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And they tell me, if you're not leaving Radun, you're going to be in the same place where your sister. He mentioned me these words. Your sister? What do you have to do with the sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know my sister is in Lida. Meanwhile, she came there, a day before me, into Radun. Where she represent papers that she's not Judy Michalowski. She's [INAUDIBLE] from Lida, a refugee from Lida. Because her house was bombed. She got, from the [INAUDIBLE] commissar, permission to settle in a neighboring village, like Radun. Specified, Radun. She came with these paper to the Judenrat. They say, don't give us the business. We know you are not Judith. We are not-- according to documents, you are Judith Michalowski [INAUDIBLE] [? doctor. ?] And we hear of you, the Ejszyszki Juden.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because from the refugees, they run away. The-- everything was going out in the neighboring shtetl from each other that they were looking for Judith. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she's Judith Michalowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's not-- I forgot the name. What'd she get? What she got? The name-- she came with the name. We came with the name. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must remind. Halpern. She's Halpern with the document. And she's a refugee from Lida from these and these streets. Her house was bombed. Oh, please, register her. She is allowed to live with the Juden in Radun.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they denied. They said, we know you are not Halpern. We know you are Judith Michalowski [INAUDIBLE] doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we don't want to have any problems like the Judenrat in Ejszyszki were of you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She is a smart girl. She was a-- a college graduate girl. She was so smart and so stupid. She went to [INAUDIBLE], to the Polish mayor, was-- were appointed from the Germans of the city. And she said, I'm a refugee of Lida. And I would like to be registered in Radun. My house was bombed. She show him the documents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says to her, I'm taking care on the Polish people and the Russian people. But not on the Jewish people. You have an Judenrat. You got-- you got to go to register to the Judenrat. So she said, I went. But they refused [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to register. He picks up the telephone. And he spoke to the Judenrat. And he says, so I have here in front of me a Halpern from Lida. Why are you refusing? You're denying her residence to give her a place to live, to register her?

speaker: interviewee
text: They say, she is not Halpern. She is Judith Michalowski from Ejszyszki. They're looking for her as Communist in Ejszyszki. And that's why we're not registered. They wouldn't let her out already. They locked her up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They locked her up.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I came to the Judenrat back, said, what did you do? What did you do? They're going to kill her now. So they said, yes. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: She got to be responsible for herself. But we don't need to be responsible, our whole community, from 1,000 people, for her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one man gave me a [INAUDIBLE] and said, go disappear. Otherwise, you'll go the same way where your-- your sister. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was the end link from Judenrat after I went through in Ejszyszki. The [INAUDIBLE] I find a shelter. But I couldn't find a shelter in Judenrat in Radun. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I ran away. They gave me a chance. I'm glad they didn't arrest me or say, there's another Ejszyszki refugee runaway. But my-- my sister, Judith, a month later, got hanged in Lida, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for running away from Ejszyszki, from running away from the authorities.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that. I went to search for her and to look for her. When they took her away to Lida, with a wagon with a horse, I was running after her halfway to Lida. [? Got, ?] by a Polish policeman, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he allowed me to run after the wagon to talk to her. She told me all the story, what she went through, how she get. And she said, don't worry. I'll manage in Lida, too, to get out, get away from them. And she gave me a name when she was in the underground to contact, one Polish man who lives in Woronow also, a neighboring shtetl, with a Jewish population like Radun, Ejszyszki. but also on this side of Russia, not in Lithuania, where the Jewish were still free, not in a ghetto locked up.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause here to change the tape.
